segments.text: Listen to a talk on a science podcast. When you think of Siberia, you picture a cold place. But in 2014, Siberia had an unusually warm summer. The temt exceeded 30 degrees Celsius a couple times. And also in 2014, a helicopter pilot flying over a particular area of Siberia spotted a strange crater, a cylindrical hole in the ground that was massive, 20 mewide and 50 medeep. Really huge. Following the report, scientists investigated and found more such craters in the same area. It looked like stuff had just exploded from the ground, with material flying out and landing hundreds of meters Res away. These explosions occurred in an area where oil and gas extraction take place. So they're a real concern. What if an explosion happens near an oil or gas pipeline? What's happening is connected to the warmer temperatures. Let's back up the region on's siis, permanently frozen. It's called permafrost with pockets of ice in it. This permafrost is on top of an extensive field of underground natural gas. The ice sts and permafrost help hold the gas under the surface, but warming temperatures cause melting the surface, so it can't seal the gas underground as effectively. Also, when the ice in the permafrost melts, it leaves behind empty cavities where the ice used to be. What's probably happening is the gas from below rises up and fills these cavities, building up the pressure. Eventually, the gas bursts through and boom a crater, causing explosion. These craters are called gas emission craters. Module 2：
